25: The Kiltie Band is the concert and marching band of Carnegie Mellon University, known for wearing kilts in recognition of Andrew Carnegie's Scottish-American ancestry. 31: The Mobot competition is an annual event at Carnegie Mellon where robots autonomously try to pass through gates, in order, and reach the finish line.
33: The institution was founded by Andrew Carnegie, and was named after him when it was established as the Carnegie Technical Schools.
34: The tradition involves students painting a specific campus landmark called "The Fence".
35: A large grassy area known as "The Cut" forms the backbone of the campus, with a separate grassy area known as "The Mall" running perpendicular. The Cut was formed by filling in a ravine (hence the name) with soil from a nearby hill that was leveled to build the College of Fine Arts building.
37: A student-run theatre organization that resides on the campus of Carnegie Mellon University. Its initial dedication was the creation and production of original musicals, but has now taken to performing both professionally published and student-written materials.
40: The College of Fine Arts (CFA) is renowned for its contributions to the arts, having educated outstanding artists, architects, designers, theater artists, and musicians who have made important contributions to culture in the United States and the world.

108: The city of Pittsburgh was named in 1758 by Scottish General John Forbes in honor of British statesman William Pitt, 1st Earl of Chatham.
114: The community of Shannopin's Town was gone by the time construction on Fort Pitt was started in 1759, and according to Stefan Lorant's book "Pittsburgh, The Story of an American City", Pittsburgh was named in 1758 by Scottish General John Forbes in honor of British statesman William Pitt, 1st Earl of Chatham.
116: During the Whiskey Rebellion, Western Pennsylvania farmers rebelled against federal taxes on whiskey. The rebellion was put down when President George Washington sent in militias from several states. The rebels marched on Pittsburgh, but the rebellion was short-lived.
117: Pittsburgh's rugged topography and dispersed natural resources of coal and gas accentuated the dispersal of industry, favoring large sites with railroad and river accessibility.
118: Pittsburgh's economy was boosted during the American Civil War with increased production of iron and armaments, especially at the Allegheny Arsenal and the Fort Pitt Foundry.
119: The Great Fire of Pittsburgh occurred on April 10, 1845. It destroyed over a thousand buildings and caused $9M in damages.
120: The Fort Pitt Blockhouse is the oldest structure in Pittsburgh, built by the British in 1764.
121: Heinz History Center is located in the Strip District, and it was previously the Chautauqua Lake Ice Company Warehouse, built in 1898. However, the question is asking about a company founded in 1869. According to the text, Henry J. Heinz founded the H.J. Heinz Company in 1869.
122: In 1901, Carnegie was 65 years of age and considering retirement. He reformed his enterprises into conventional joint stock corporations as preparation for this. That same year, J. P. Morgan merged Carnegie Steel Company and several other companies into U.S. Steel.
123: Pittsburgh's economy diversified after World War II, with a greater emphasis on light industries. The city also successfully shifted its economy to focus on education, tourism, and services, including healthcare, finance, and high technology such as robotics.
124: The "Renaissance" is a period in Pittsburgh's history that refers to a clean air and civic revitalization project launched in 1945, and a cultural and neighborhood development project launched in 1977.
128: The park is located at the confluence of the Allegheny and Monongahela rivers, where the outlines of Fort Duquesne and Fort Pitt have been laid in granite slabs.
132: One of the most serious labor confrontations in U.S. history, lasting 143 days in 1892, centered on Carnegie Steel's main plant in Homestead, Pennsylvania, and grew into a national labor movement issue.

137: The Mexican War Streets neighborhood, along with Allegheny West and Manchester, are known for their rich architectural heritage.
138: A 15-acre botanical garden with extensive greenhouses, located in the city's history as an early-20th-century industrial capital.
140: The Carnegie Science Center (1991), which now also houses the Henry Buhl, Jr., Planetarium and Observatory (1939).
141: Andrew Carnegie was a Scottish-American industrialist and philanthropist. He led the expansion of the American steel industry and became one of the richest Americans in history. He played a key role in the development of the U.S. steel industry and founded the Carnegie Steel Company. He was also a prominent philanthropist, donating millions to charities, foundations, and universities, including the establishment of the Carnegie Technical Schools (now Carnegie Mellon University) in Pittsburgh, which provided vocational training for working-class Pittsburghers.

144: The Nationality Rooms program at the University of Pittsburgh is a collection of classrooms located in the Cathedral of Learning that showcase pre-19th-century learning environments from around the world.
146: The Smithfield Street Bridge was the world's first lenticular truss bridge and is one of the bridges that contributed to Pittsburgh being called the "City of bridges".
147: Primanti Bros. sandwiches, specifically those with meat, cheese, hand-cut French fries, tomatoes, and coleslaw between two slices of Italian bread.
150: Pittsburgh's economy has shifted from heavy industry to services, medicine, higher education, tourism, banking, corporate headquarters, and high technology. The region's technology industries have a significant annual payroll, with over 1,600 technology companies in 2010. The top two private employers in the city are the University of Pittsburgh Medical Center and the West Penn Allegheny Health System.
152: The Golden Triangle is the business district in Pittsburgh where the Allegheny and Monongahela rivers unite to form the Ohio River, and it is also a nickname for the downtown area.
153: Duquesne University Red Masquers, founded in 1912.
155: Home to 4,000 animals, including many endangered species.
156: The Roberto Clemente Bridge was renamed in honor of the former Pirate, Roberto Clemente. The bridge was initially called the 6th Street Bridge. According to the Wikipedia article on PNC Park, the city of Pittsburgh renamed the bridge after a popular sentiment by fans for the Pirates to name the stadium after Clemente. The renaming occurred after a compromise to fans who called the use of tax dollars "corporate welfare" while others said the Pirates should contribute more than their pledged $40 million.
157: The Strip District is famous for its open-air marketplace by day and a clubbing destination by night. It is also known for its multicultural traditions, showcasing the city's diversity, and is home to many restaurants and clubs that reflect this diversity.
